The algorithm below is used to simulate the results of flipping a coin 4 times. Consider the goal of determining
whether the simulation resulted in an equal number of heads and tails.
Step 1 :
Initialize the variables heads_counter and flip_counter to 0.
Step 2 :
A variable coin_flip is randomly assigned a value of either 0 or 1.
If coin_flip has the value 0, the coin flip result is heads,
so heads_counter is incremented by 1.

Increment the value of flip_counter by 1.
Step 4 :
Repeat steps 2 and 3 until flip_counter equals 4.

subject: AP Computer Science Principles
text:
Two lists, list1 and list2, contain the names of books found in two different collections. A librarian wants
to create newList, which will contain the names of all books found in either list, in alphabetical order, with
duplicate entries removed.
For example, if list1 contains
["Macbeth", "Frankenstein", "Jane Eyre"]
and list2 contains
["Frankenstein", "Dracula", "Macbeth", "Hamlet"],
then newList will contain
["Dracula", "Frankenstein", "Hamlet", "Jane Eyre", "Macbeth"].
The following procedures are available to create newList.
Procedure
Explanation
Sort (list)
Sorts list in alphabetical order and returns
the resulting list.
Combine (list1, list2)
Creates a new list consisting of the entries from
list1 followed by the entries from list2.
The resulting list is returned.
RemoveDuplicates (list) Iterates through list. If any two or more
entries have the same value, the duplicate entries
are removed so that any entry appears at most
once. The resulting list is returned.
Which of the following code segments will correctly create newList?

options.A:
```text
newList <- Combine(list1, list2)
newList <- Sort(newList)
newList <- RemoveDuplicates(newList)
```
options.B:
```text
list1 <- Sort(list1)
list2 <- Sort(list2)
newList <- Combine(list1, list2)
newList <- RemoveDuplicates(newList)
```
options.C:
```text
list1 <- RemoveDuplicates(list1)
list2 <- RemoveDuplicates(list2)
newList <- Combine(list1, list2)
newList <- Sort(newList)
```
options.D:
```text
list1 <- RemoveDuplicates(list1)
list1 <- Sort(list1)
list2 <- RemoveDuplicates(list2)
list2 <- Sort(list2)
newList <- Combine(list1, list2)
```

and 3, the result is 0. Which of the following is the best explanation for the result?

options.A: An overflow error occurred.
options.B: A round-off error occurred.
options.C: The result was affected by lossy data compression.
options.D: The result was approximated by a floating-point representation.

and produces a single output.
If the inputs A and C are both true, which of the following best describes the output of the AND gate?

options.B: The output will be false no matter what the value of input B is.
options.C: The output will be true if input B is true; otherwise it will be false.
options.D: The output will be false if input B is true; otherwise it will be true.

subject: AP Computer Science Principles
text:
A certain computer game is played between a human player and a computer-controlled player. Every time the
computer-controlled player has a turn, the game runs slowly because the computer evaluates all potential moves
and selects the best one. Which of the following best describes the possibility of improving the running speed of
the game?

options.A:
The game’s running speed can only be improved if the game is played between two human players instead of
with the computer-controlled player.
options.B:
The game’s running speed might be improved by using a process that finds approximate solutions every time
the computer-controlled player has a turn.
options.C:
The game’s running speed cannot be improved because computers can only be programmed to find the best
possible solution.
options.D:
The game’s running speed cannot be improved because the game is an example of an algorithm that does not
run in a reasonable time.

subject: AP Computer Science Principles
text:
Many Web browsers allow users to open anonymous windows. During a browsing session in an anonymous
window, the browser does not record a browsing history or a list of downloaded files. When the anonymous
window is exited, cookies created during the session are deleted. Which of the following statements about browsing
sessions in an anonymous window is true?

options.A:
The activities of a user browsing in an anonymous window will not be visible to people who monitor the
user’s network, such as the system administrator.
options.B:
Items placed in a Web store’s shopping cart for future purchase during the anonymous browsing session will
not be saved on the user’s computer.
options.C: A user will not be able to log in to e-mail or social media accounts during the anonymous browsing session.
options.D:
A user browsing in an anonymous window will be protected from viruses launched from any Web sites visited
or files downloaded.

subject: AP Computer Science Principles
text: Which of the following best describes a Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack?

options.A: An attempt by a country to deny its citizens access to the Internet
options.B:
An attempt to deny users access to a Web site’s resources by flooding the Web site with requests from
multiple systems
options.C: An attempt by one user to deny service to another user by posting material on a social network
options.D: An attempt by a user of the Internet to get private information from a secure database

subject: AP Computer Science Principles
text: A program is expressed in a programming language. Which of the following is true of the program?

options.A:
The program can also be expressed as binary code, but will be more easily understood by humans when
expressed in a higher-level programming language.
options.B: The program can also be expressed as binary code, which will reduce the likelihood of errors.
options.C: The program cannot be expressed as binary code, because binary code can only be used to represent data.
options.D:
Some parts of the program can be expressed as binary code, but operations must be expressed using a
higher-level programming language.

subject: AP Computer Science Principles

A programmer is deciding between using a linear or binary search to find a target value in a sorted list. Which of
the following is true?

options.A: In all cases, a binary search of a sorted list requires fewer comparisons than a linear search.
options.B: Generally, the advantage of using a binary search over a linear search increases as the size of the list increases.
options.C:
A linear search will generally run faster than a binary search because a linear search requires fewer lines of
code to implement.
options.D:
Using a linear search is preferable to using a binary search if there is a chance that the target may not be found
in the list.

subject: AP Computer Science Principles
text:
The procedure DrawCircle (x, y, r) can be used to draw a circle on a coordinate grid. The circle is
centered at the coordinate (x, y) and has a radius of r units. The procedure will be used to draw the
following figure on a coordinate grid.
Which of the following code segments can be used to draw the figure?

options.A:
```text
xPos <- 3
yPos <- 6
REPEAT 3 TIMES
{
  DrawCircle(xPos, yPos, 2)
  xPos <- xPos + 2
}
yPos <- yPos + 2
```
options.B:
```text
xPos <- 3
yPos <- 6
REPEAT 3 TIMES
{
  DrawCircle(xPos, yPos, 2)
  xPos <- xPos + 2
}
yPos <- yPos - 2
```
options.C:
```text
xPos <- 7
yPos <- 2
REPEAT 3 TIMES
{
  DrawCircle(xPos, yPos, 2)
  xPos <- xPos + 2
  yPos <- yPos + 2
}
```
options.D:
```text
xPos <- 7
yPos <- 2
REPEAT 3 TIMES
{
  DrawCircle(xPos, yPos, 2)
  xPos <- xPos + 2
  yPos <- yPos - 2
}
```

purchases a customer has made since enrolling in the frequent customer program, including his or her first
purchase.

enrolling in the frequent customer program?

```text
count <- GetCount(customerID)
IF (count / 10 = 0)
{
  cost <- 0
}
```
options.B:
```text
count <- GetCount(customerID)
IF (NOT (count / 10 = 0))
{
  cost <- 0
}
```
options.C:
```text
count <- GetCount(customerID)
IF (count MOD 10 = 0)
{
  cost <- 0
}
```

count <- GetCount(customerID)

{

}
